summarization.a: 3D schematic of the Cambridge NanoTech Savannah S100 ALD system used for Al₂O₃ deposition with TMA and water precursors. Key components include precursor cylinders at the bottom, stop valves for millisecond pulse control, a carrier gas manifold for nitrogen flow (20 sccm), a heated circular chamber (150 mm diameter, 100–250°C range), and an outlet to a vacuum pump maintaining ~0.30 Torr background pressure.

a.question: How are the TMA and water precursors delivered into the chamber?
a.answer: Precursors are stored in separate cylinders at room temperature and injected by their own vapor pressure (9.43 Torr for TMA, 23.68 Torr for water). Stop valves control brief 0.015 s pulses, while nitrogen carrier gas continuously flows through the system and a vacuum pump maintains low background pressure.

a.question: What is the temperature display on the chamber showing in this figure?
Can this system accommodate 4-inch wafers?
a.answer: Yes. The chamber is designed for substrates up to 100 mm diameter.

a.question: What is the role of the carrier gas manifold in this ALD system?
a.answer: (1) Transport precursor vapors into the chamber, (2) purge excess precursors between pulses, and (3) remove reaction byproducts (CH₄) during the purge steps.
